summary: Gruber analyzes Nokia's decision to abandon MeeGo in favor of Windows Phone, prompted by the impressive N9 demo. He argues that if MeeGo is genuinely as good as the demo suggests, Nokia's pivot to Windows Phone could be a tragic, possibly catastrophic mistake. However, he acknowledges that demos can be misleading and that the real issue may lie in MeeGo's developer tools and third-party app ecosystem. He considers whether CEO Stephen Elop concluded MeeGo would never attract a competitive app library. Either way, Gruber frames this as evidence of how deep a hole Nokia has dug for itself — the situation looks bad regardless of which interpretation is correct.
key_points: The N9 demo makes MeeGo look like a credible top-shelf smartphone contender, raising questions about why Nokia would abandon it for Windows Phone
A large app library matters not because users need hundreds of apps, but because breadth increases the odds that any individual user can find their specific 10 must-have apps
Nokia's situation is a lose-lose: if MeeGo is good, abandoning it is tragic; if it's not, Nokia wasted years of development
Shipping the N9 while betting the company on Windows Phone risks confusing and misleading Nokia's customers
Platform success requires being noticeably better in some significant way than existing competitors, not just adequate
money_quotes: If MeeGo is as good as Nokia's demo makes it look, then it strikes me as a tragic — possibly catastrophic — mistake for Nokia to abandon it in favor of Windows Phone.
The whole point of these app phones is that you can add software to them.
Your killer apps are not necessarily my killer apps.

What a new mobile platform needs to succeed, to come out of the gate and make a dent in the market, is to be noticeably better in some significant way — or even better, ways, plural — than those platforms already on the market.
Platforms and developer tools are hard.
If the N9 is as good as it looks in the demo, why is Nokia betting on Windows Phone rather than MeeGo? And if it's not as good as it looks, what has Nokia been doing for the past four years?
How does this do anything other than confuse — and, arguably, mislead — their customers?
key_insight: Nokia's decision to ship the impressive N9 while simultaneously abandoning MeeGo for Windows Phone reveals a company in strategic disarray — the situation looks damning no matter which way you read it.
